# Paper Chat

> Interactive paper reader with AI-powered Q&A and live simulations

## Figure Generation

The assistant can generate four types of publication-quality figures on demand. Each figure runs a simulation with your specified parameters and renders the results with matplotlib.

### Figure Types

#### Calibration Curve

**Command:** `generate_figure(figure_type="calibration")`

Plots predicted probability vs observed frequency for all three mechanisms (E, C, R). A perfectly calibrated forecaster would lie on the diagonal.

* X-axis: Predicted probability (binned)
* Y-axis: Observed frequency
* Three lines: Equal (green), Capital (red), Reputation (blue)
* Diagonal reference line for perfect calibration

This is the primary visualization for comparing mechanism accuracy.

#### Reputation Separation

**Command:** `generate_figure(figure_type="reputation_separation")`

Boxplot showing the final reputation distributions for good vs bad agents.

* Two boxes: "Good agents" and "Bad agents"
* Y-axis: Final reputation value
* Shows median, quartiles, and outliers

A clear separation between the boxes indicates the reputation system is successfully distinguishing skilled from unskilled forecasters.

#### Learning Dynamics

**Command:** `generate_figure(figure_type="learning_dynamics")`

Rolling Brier score (window = 100 questions) plotted over all J questions.

* X-axis: Question number
* Y-axis: Rolling mean Brier score
* Three lines: E, C, R
* Shows how each mechanism's accuracy evolves as reputations converge

Early in the simulation, all three mechanisms perform similarly. As reputations separate, R should diverge from C (especially in Regime B).

#### Inequality Sensitivity

**Command:** `generate_figure(figure_type="inequality_sensitivity")`

Two-panel scatter plot showing how stake dispersion (σ\_s) affects outcomes:

* **Panel (a):** R vs C improvement (%) as a function of σ\_s
* **Panel (b):** C vs E distortion (%) as a function of σ\_s

Uses pre-computed sweep data across multiple σ\_s values with error bars from multi-seed runs. This figure does not require regime or alpha parameters since it aggregates across the full sweep.

### Color Scheme

All figures use a consistent color scheme:

| Mechanism      | Color             | Marker  |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- |
| Equal (E)      | Green (`#2ca02c`) | Diamond |
| Capital (C)    | Red (`#d62728`)   | Circle  |
| Reputation (R) | Blue (`#1f77b4`)  | Square  |

### How Figures Are Delivered

1. The agent calls `generate_figure` with the desired type and parameters
2. The backend runs a single-seed simulation (or loads sweep data for inequality sensitivity)
3. Matplotlib renders the figure and saves it as a PNG
4. The image is encoded as a base64 data URI and added to `pending_images`
5. After the agent finishes responding, the image is injected into the chat stream
6. The PNG is also saved to `data/figures/` with a UUID filename for static serving

### Customization

You can control figure parameters through natural language:

* *"Generate a calibration figure for Regime B with α = 1.5"*
* *"Show reputation separation with σ\_bad = 2.0"*
* *"Plot learning dynamics for the correlated regime"*

The agent maps your request to the appropriate `generate_figure` call with the right parameter values.


## Running Simulations

Paper Chat includes a live simulation engine that can run Monte Carlo experiments on demand through the chat interface.

### Overview

The simulation models a prediction market with N agents making forecasts over J binary questions. Three aggregation mechanisms compete:

| Mechanism           | Symbol | Formula                                  |
| ------------------- | ------ | ---------------------------------------- |
| Equal-weighted      | E      | Simple average of all predictions        |
| Capital-weighted    | C      | Weighted by each agent's stake           |
| Reputation-weighted | R      | Weighted by reputation × stake (rᵢ · sᵢ) |

### Wealth-Skill Regimes

The key variable is how wealth (stake) correlates with forecasting skill:

| Regime | Name            | Description                                         |
| ------ | --------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| A      | Uncorrelated    | Stakes randomly assigned — no relationship to skill |
| B      | Anti-correlated | \~80% chance high-stake agents are bad forecasters  |
| C      | Correlated      | \~80% chance high-stake agents are good forecasters |

Regime B is the adversarial case where capital distortion is worst. Regime C is the favorable case where money tracks skill.

### Parameters

The simulation has 11 configurable parameters (defaults shown):

| Parameter          | Symbol  | Default | Description                             |
| ------------------ | ------- | ------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Agents             | N       | 50      | Number of forecasting agents            |
| Questions          | J       | 5,000   | Binary questions per campaign           |
| Tail index         | α       | 1.7     | Pareto exponent for wealth distribution |
| Wealth scale       | β       | 1.0     | Scale parameter for stakes              |
| Good agent noise   | σ\_good | 0.6     | Signal noise for skilled agents         |
| Bad agent noise    | σ\_bad  | 1.6     | Signal noise for unskilled agents       |
| Good fraction      | f\_good | 0.2     | Proportion of skilled agents (20%)      |
| Stake dispersion   | σ\_s    | 1.0     | Lognormal spread of stakes              |
| Initial reputation | r₀      | 1.0     | Starting reputation for all agents      |
| Learning rate      | λ       | 0.05    | Reputation update speed                 |
| Penalty steepness  | k       | 5.0     | Exponential penalty for inaccuracy      |

### Agent Types

Each simulation has two types of agents:

* **Good agents** (20% by default) — observe a noisy signal with σ\_good = 0.6
* **Bad agents** (80% by default) — observe a noisier signal with σ\_bad = 1.6

Both apply a sigmoid function to their noisy signal to produce a probability estimate. A bias term is calibrated via `tune_bias()` so that the mean prediction matches a 10% base rate.

### Reputation Update

After each question, every agent's reputation updates:

```
r_i ← (1 − λ) · r_i + λ · exp(−k · (p_ij − Y_j)²)
```

Good agents converge to higher reputations over time because their predictions are closer to outcomes. The learning rate λ controls how quickly this happens, and the steepness k determines how harshly inaccurate predictions are penalized.

### Using Simulations in Chat

Ask the assistant to run a simulation by specifying parameters:

* *"Run a simulation with α = 1.5 and σ\_s = 2.0 in Regime B"*
* *"What happens if we increase the bad agent noise to 2.0?"*
* *"Compare Regime A and B with 10 seeds"*

The agent calls `run_custom_simulation` which runs up to 10 seeds with a cap of 5,000 questions each, then returns aggregated results including mean Brier scores, R vs C improvement, and reputation separation.

### Pre-Computed Results

For the paper's main analysis, results from 630 runs (3 regimes × 210 parameter combinations) are pre-computed in CSV files. The `query_results` and `compare_mechanisms` tools access these without re-running simulations, making lookups instant.


## Navigation & Progress

Paper Chat tracks your position in the paper and provides multiple navigation aids.

### Table of Contents

A fixed sidebar on the left lists all 8 paper sections:

1. Introduction
2. Related Work
3. Model & Methods
4. Results
5. Discussion
6. Limitations & Future Work
7. Conclusion
8. References

Click any section to smooth-scroll to it. The active section is highlighted with a left teal border and bold text.

#### Animations

TOC items fade in with a staggered animation (40ms delay between items) when the component mounts, giving a cascading entrance effect.

### Section Tracking

The `useIntersectionTracker` hook uses the Intersection Observer API to detect which section is currently in view:

* **Root margin:** `-20% 0px -70% 0px` — a section is considered "active" when its top edge enters the upper 20% of the viewport
* **Threshold:** `0` — triggers as soon as any part of the section crosses the margin

All `[data-section]` elements are observed. When a new section enters the detection zone, the context updates and three things happen simultaneously:

1. The **TOC** highlights the new section
2. The **header** updates the section label with a fade animation
3. The **suggested prompts** below the chat change to match the section

### Reading Progress Bar

A 2px teal bar at the top of the paper panel shows how far you've scrolled. It's computed as a fraction of total scroll height:

```
progress = scrollTop / (scrollHeight - clientHeight)
```

The bar width scales from 0% to 100% as you scroll through the paper.

### Scroll-to-Section

When you click a TOC entry, the app calls `scrollToSection(slug)` from the paper reader context. This:

1. Finds the `[data-section="slug"]` element
2. Scrolls the paper panel container to bring it into view with `scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' })`
3. The intersection observer then picks up the change and updates the active state


## Text Selection

Select any passage in the paper and ask the AI about it — without typing a prompt from scratch.

### How It Works

1. **Select text** — highlight any passage longer than 3 characters in the Markdown view
2. **Tooltip appears** — a floating "Ask about this" button positions itself above the selection
3. **Click** — the selected text is sent to the chat composer as a prefilled prompt
4. **Chat activates** — the composer focuses with the passage quoted, ready to send or edit

### Selection Tooltip

The `SelectionTooltip` component listens for `mouseup` events inside the paper container. When it detects a selection:

* It reads the selection text via `window.getSelection()`
* Filters out selections shorter than 3 characters
* Computes the tooltip position from the selection's bounding rect
* Renders the tooltip centered above the selection with a fade-in animation

The tooltip dismisses when:

* You click outside the selection
* The selection is cleared
* You click the "Ask about this" button (after sending to chat)

### Auto-Compose

When you click "Ask about this", the app constructs a prompt:

```
Regarding this passage: "{selected text}" —
```

The text is truncated to 300 characters if the selection is long. This prompt is set as the composer value via `sendToChat()` in the paper reader context, which calls the registered `chatMethods.setComposerValue()`.

You can then:

* **Send immediately** — the AI will interpret the passage in context
* **Edit first** — append your specific question after the quoted passage

### Integration with Context

The selection flow connects the paper reader to the chat through the `PaperReaderContext`:

```
SelectionTooltip → sendToChat(text) → PaperReaderContext → chatMethods.setComposerValue()
```

This avoids tight coupling between components — the tooltip doesn't know about ChatKit directly, and the chat panel doesn't know about text selection. The context bridges them.

:::info
Text selection only works in **Markdown view**. In PDF mode, the text layer supports native browser selection but does not trigger the tooltip.
:::


## Reading Views

Paper Chat offers two ways to read the paper: a rendered **Markdown view** with full LaTeX math support, and the **original PDF**.

### View Toggle

A two-button toggle in the paper panel header switches between modes. The active mode is highlighted with the teal accent color.

```
┌──────────────┬──────────────┐
│ Reading View │     PDF      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┘
```

The view mode is stored in the `PaperReaderContext` and persists as you navigate between sections.

### Markdown View

The default reading experience renders each section through `react-markdown` with:

* **remarkMath** + **rehypeKatex** — inline and display LaTeX equations render natively
* **remarkGfm** — GitHub-Flavored Markdown for tables, strikethrough, and task lists
* Auto-slugified heading IDs for deep linking (e.g., `#reputation-update-rule`)
* Lazy-loaded figures with captions extracted from `alt` text
* Responsive table wrappers that scroll horizontally on small screens

#### Typography

The Markdown view uses the paper's design system:

| Role        | Font               |
| ----------- | ------------------ |
| Headings    | IBM Plex Sans      |
| Body text   | Newsreader (serif) |
| UI elements | DM Sans            |
| Code        | SF Mono            |

#### Skeleton Loading

While the paper content loads from `/api/paper/full`, animated skeleton blocks show the page structure — title, paragraphs, and figures — so the layout doesn't jump when content arrives.

### PDF View

Toggle to PDF mode to see the original formatted paper. The PDF viewer uses `react-pdf` and renders:

* All pages at 700px width with text and annotation layers
* Page numbers below each page
* Full scroll through the entire document
* Loading and error states

The PDF is served from `/api/paper/pdf` on the backend.

### When to Use Each View

|                      | Markdown                           | PDF                      |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| **Math rendering**   | Inline KaTeX                       | Embedded in PDF          |
| **Text selection**   | Yes — triggers tooltip             | Limited (PDF text layer) |
| **Section tracking** | Yes — updates TOC, header, prompts | No                       |
| **Navigation**       | TOC + smooth scroll                | Page-by-page scroll      |
| **Figures**          | Loaded from server                 | Embedded in PDF          |

The Markdown view is recommended for interactive use since it integrates with the TOC, selection tooltip, and contextual prompts.


## Conversational Q\&A

The right panel hosts an AI research assistant that can answer questions about the paper, run simulations, and generate figures — all through natural conversation.

### ChatKit Integration

The chat interface is built on [OpenAI ChatKit](https://github.com/openai/chatkit), which provides:

* Thread-based conversation management
* Streaming response rendering
* Markdown and code block formatting
* A composable React component library

The frontend uses `@openai/chatkit-react` and the backend implements a `ChatKitServer` that routes messages to the paper agent.

### Start Screen

When you first open Paper Chat, the assistant greets you with:

> "Ask me anything about the paper."

Below the greeting, five suggested prompts help you get started:

* Summarize the paper's key contributions
* Explain the three aggregation mechanisms
* Show the main results table
* Run a simulation with custom parameters
* Generate a calibration figure

### Contextual Suggested Prompts

As you scroll through different sections of the paper, the suggested prompts below the chat update to match. Each section has 3–4 curated prompts:

| Section         | Example Prompts                                             |
| --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Introduction    | "What cultural breakout markets is the paper targeting?"    |
| Model & Methods | "Explain the reputation update rule step by step"           |
| Results         | "Compare Regime B results across all three mechanisms"      |
| Discussion      | "What does the multiplicative limitation mean in practice?" |
| References      | "What are the key prediction markets papers cited?"         |

Prompts are defined in `contextual-prompts.ts` and retrieved via `getPromptsForSection(slug)`. If the current section doesn't have specific prompts, it falls back to the Introduction set.

### Theme

The chat panel uses a custom ChatKit theme matching the app's design:

| Property      | Value            |
| ------------- | ---------------- |
| Color scheme  | Light            |
| Accent color  | `#2d7a7a` (teal) |
| Font          | DM Sans          |
| Font size     | 15px             |
| Border radius | Soft             |
| Density       | Normal           |

### Message Flow

1. User types or selects a suggested prompt
2. ChatKit sends a POST to `/chatkit`
3. The backend `PaperChatKitServer.respond()` runs the agent
4. The agent calls tools as needed (search, simulate, generate figures)
5. Responses stream back as ChatKit events
6. Any generated figures are injected at the end of the response

The agent is allowed up to 25 turns per request, meaning it can chain multiple tool calls to answer complex questions.


## Thinking Indicator

When the agent is processing a request, a thinking indicator shows what phase of reasoning it's in and how long it's been working.

### Phases

The indicator cycles through six labeled phases as the agent works:

| Phase | Label            |
| ----- | ---------------- |
| 1     | Reading...       |
| 2     | Searching...     |
| 3     | Analyzing...     |
| 4     | Reasoning...     |
| 5     | Synthesizing...  |
| 6     | Deep thinking... |

Phases advance on a timer to give visual feedback even when the agent is waiting on a single long operation. The elapsed time (in seconds) is shown alongside the phase label.

### Hourglass Animation

The thinking indicator features a pixel-art hourglass rendered on a 16x20 HTML canvas. The animation combines two effects:

* **Rotation** — the hourglass rotates 360 degrees over 2 seconds (`hg-spin`)
* **Fade pulse** — the opacity pulses gently over 2.5 seconds (`hg-fade`)

This creates a subtle, non-distracting animation that signals activity without pulling focus from the surrounding content.

### When It Appears

The thinking indicator is visible whenever the agent is:

* Executing tool calls (paper search, simulation, figure generation)
* Waiting for the model to generate a response
* Processing a multi-turn chain of tool calls

It disappears as soon as the response begins streaming. For fast queries that complete in under a second, the indicator may flash briefly or not appear at all.

### Streaming Responses

Once the agent has finished reasoning and tool execution, the response streams into the chat. ChatKit handles the streaming display — text appears incrementally as tokens arrive from the backend.

If the agent generates figures during processing, the images are injected at the end of the streamed response (after all text content), since the figure data URIs are collected in a `pending_images` list and flushed after the agent completes.


## Agent Tools

The paper assistant has 8 specialized tools that let it go beyond text retrieval — searching, computing, simulating, and visualizing on demand.

### Paper Tools

#### `search_paper`

Full-text search across the paper using TF-IDF ranking.

| Parameter | Type  | Default | Description                         |
| --------- | ----- | ------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `query`   | `str` | —       | Search query                        |
| `top_k`   | `int` | `3`     | Max results to return (capped at 5) |

Returns ranked passages with relevance scores. Only results scoring above 0.01 are included. The paper is pre-indexed into paragraph-level chunks (minimum 50 characters each) using scikit-learn's `TfidfVectorizer` with 5,000 max features and English stop words removed.

#### `get_section`

Retrieves the full content of a paper section by name.

| Parameter | Type  | Description                                    |
| --------- | ----- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `section` | `str` | Section name (case-insensitive, fuzzy-matched) |

Returns up to 3,000 characters of the matched section. Supports flexible input — "results", "Results", or "RESULTS" all match.

#### `get_references`

Searches the paper's BibTeX bibliography.

| Parameter | Type  | Description                                    |
| --------- | ----- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `query`   | `str` | Search term to match against reference entries |

Returns up to 10 matching BibTeX entries. Useful for finding specific citations, author names, or publication details.

### Simulation Tools

#### `query_results`

Looks up pre-computed simulation results from the main results table (630 runs).

| Parameter | Type  | Default   | Description                                                          |
| --------- | ----- | --------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `regime`  | `str` | —         | `"A"` (uncorrelated), `"B"` (anti-correlated), or `"C"` (correlated) |
| `metric`  | `str` | `"Brier"` | Metric to query                                                      |

Returns: R vs C improvement (%), confidence interval, p-value, C vs E delta (%), reputation separation ratio, number of questions, and seed count.

#### `compare_mechanisms`

Returns per-seed breakdown or inequality sweep data for a regime.

| Parameter | Type    | Default | Description                |
| --------- | ------- | ------- | -------------------------- |
| `regime`  | `str`   | —       | Regime identifier          |
| `sigma_s` | `float` | `1.0`   | Stake dispersion parameter |

Returns a table of 10 seeds showing Brier scores for equal-weighted (E), capital-weighted (C), and reputation-weighted (R) mechanisms, plus R vs C improvement per seed.

#### `get_parameters`

Returns the default simulation parameter table — no arguments needed.

| Parameter | Default | Description             |
| --------- | ------- | ----------------------- |
| N         | 50      | Number of agents        |
| J         | 5,000   | Questions per campaign  |
| α         | 1.7     | Pareto tail index       |
| β         | 1.0     | Wealth scale            |
| σ\_good   | 0.6     | Good agent noise        |
| σ\_bad    | 1.6     | Bad agent noise         |
| f\_good   | 0.2     | Fraction of good agents |
| σ\_s      | 1.0     | Stake dispersion        |
| r₀        | 1.0     | Initial reputation      |
| λ         | 0.05    | Learning rate           |
| k         | 5.0     | Penalty steepness       |

#### `run_custom_simulation`

Runs a live Monte Carlo simulation with custom parameters.

| Parameter   | Type    | Default | Description              |
| ----------- | ------- | ------- | ------------------------ |
| `alpha`     | `float` | —       | Pareto tail index        |
| `sigma_s`   | `float` | —       | Stake dispersion         |
| `regime`    | `str`   | —       | Wealth-skill regime      |
| `n_seeds`   | `int`   | `5`     | Number of seeds (max 10) |
| `sigma_bad` | `float` | `1.6`   | Bad agent noise          |

Returns aggregated results: mean Brier scores for E/C/R, R vs C improvement (%), C vs E delta (%), and reputation separation. Capped at 5,000 questions per campaign for performance.

#### `generate_figure`

Renders a publication-quality matplotlib figure and returns it as a markdown image.

| Parameter     | Type    | Default | Description                |
| ------------- | ------- | ------- | -------------------------- |
| `figure_type` | `str`   | —       | One of 4 types (see below) |
| `regime`      | `str`   | `"A"`   | Regime for the figure      |
| `alpha`       | `float` | `1.7`   | Tail index                 |
| `sigma_s`     | `float` | `1.0`   | Stake dispersion           |
| `sigma_bad`   | `float` | `1.6`   | Bad agent noise            |

**Figure types:**

| Type                     | Description                                               |
| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `calibration`            | Predicted vs observed probability curve for E, C, and R   |
| `reputation_separation`  | Boxplot of good vs bad agent final reputations            |
| `learning_dynamics`      | Rolling Brier score (window=100) over all questions       |
| `inequality_sensitivity` | 2-panel: R vs C improvement and C vs E distortion vs σ\_s |

Figures are saved to disk and also returned as base64 data URIs injected into the chat stream. See [Figure Generation](/simulation/figures) for details on each plot type.

### Tool Usage Guidelines

The agent is instructed to:

* Limit tool calls to 2–3 per question
* Cite specific paper sections in responses
* Use LaTeX notation for equations
* Prefer `search_paper` for factual lookups and `get_section` for broader context


## Backend Architecture

The backend is a FastAPI application that serves paper content, handles chat conversations, and runs simulations.

### Tech Stack

| Library           | Purpose                       |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------- |
| FastAPI 0.115+    | HTTP framework                |
| OpenAI Agents SDK | Agent orchestration (o4-mini) |
| OpenAI ChatKit    | Chat/thread management        |
| NumPy, SciPy      | Numerical simulation          |
| Matplotlib        | Figure generation             |
| Pandas            | Data loading and manipulation |
| scikit-learn      | TF-IDF search indexing        |

### Endpoints

| Endpoint          | Method | Description                                                               |
| ----------------- | ------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `/`               | GET    | Health check — returns `{"status": "ok", "agent": "Paper Assistant"}`     |
| `/api/paper/full` | GET    | Returns all 8 paper sections as JSON with slug, title, content, and order |
| `/api/paper/pdf`  | GET    | Serves the paper PDF file                                                 |
| `/chatkit`        | POST   | ChatKit request handler — routes to the paper agent                       |
| `/figures`        | MOUNT  | Static file serving for generated PNG figures                             |

CORS is enabled for all origins to support the Vite dev server proxy.

### Paper Content

The paper is stored as 8 Markdown files (`*_draft.md`) in `app/data/paper/`. On startup, the server:

1. Loads each file and assigns it a section slug, title, and order
2. Replaces figure placeholders with Markdown image syntax pointing to `/figures/`
3. Strips per-section reference lists (references are unified in the final section)

The 8 sections: Introduction, Related Work, Model & Methods, Results, Discussion, Limitations & Future Work, Conclusion, References.

### ChatKit Server

`PaperChatKitServer` extends ChatKit's `ChatKitServer` base class:

* `respond()` is an async generator that handles incoming user messages
* It instantiates the paper agent and runs it with up to 25 turns
* Responses stream back as `ThreadItemAddedEvent` and `ThreadItemDoneEvent`
* After the agent completes, any pending figure images (base64 data URIs) are injected into the stream

### Agent Configuration

The paper agent uses OpenAI's Agents SDK with:

* **Model:** o4-mini
* **Name:** Paper Assistant
* **Instructions:** \~3,000 lines covering paper content, equations, findings, and behavioral guidelines
* **Tools:** 8 function tools (see [Agent Tools](/chat/tools))
* **Behavior:** Thoughtful research collaborator that reasons beyond explicit claims, cites sections, and limits tool calls to 2–3 per question

### In-Memory Store

`MemoryStore` implements ChatKit's `Store` interface for thread and message persistence:

* **Threads** — conversation sessions with UUID-based IDs
* **Thread items** — individual messages and responses
* **Attachments** — file storage for the chat

This is an MVP store. All data lives in memory and is lost on restart. It can be replaced with a database-backed implementation by swapping the store class.

### Data Files

```
app/data/
├── paper/
│   ├── introduction_draft.md
│   ├── related_work_draft.md
│   ├── model_methods_draft.md
│   ├── results_draft.md
│   ├── discussion_draft.md
│   ├── limitations_draft.md
│   ├── conclusion_draft.md
│   └── references_draft.md
├── csv/
│   ├── scenius_main_results.csv
│   ├── scenius_main_perseed.csv
│   ├── scenius_sweep_results.csv
│   └── inequality_sweep_results.csv
└── figures/          ← generated PNGs written here
```

### Deployment

The backend includes a Dockerfile and `railway.toml` for deployment on Railway:

* Default port: 8321
* Entry point: `uvicorn app.main:app`
* Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable


## Frontend Architecture

The frontend is a React 19 single-page application built with Vite and TypeScript.

### Tech Stack

| Library                    | Version | Purpose                   |
| -------------------------- | ------- | ------------------------- |
| React                      | 19      | UI framework              |
| TypeScript                 | 5.7     | Type safety               |
| Vite                       | 6       | Build tool and dev server |
| @openai/chatkit-react      | 1.5     | Chat UI components        |
| react-markdown             | 10.1    | Markdown rendering        |
| react-pdf                  | 10.4    | PDF viewer                |
| remark-math + rehype-katex | —       | LaTeX math rendering      |
| KaTeX                      | 0.16    | Math typesetting engine   |

### Component Tree

```
App
├── PaperReaderProvider (context)
│   ├── Header
│   │   └── Section label (animated)
│   └── SplitLayout
│       ├── PaperPanel (left)
│       │   ├── View toggle (Markdown / PDF)
│       │   ├── Reading progress bar
│       │   ├── TableOfContents
│       │   ├── PaperSection × 8 (via ReactMarkdown)
│       │   ├── SelectionTooltip
│       │   └── PdfViewer (alternate view)
│       └── ChatPanel (right)
│           ├── ChatKit
│           ├── ThinkingIndicator
│           └── SuggestedPrompts
```

### Split Layout

The `SplitLayout` component renders a draggable two-column layout:

* Default split: 65% left (paper), 35% right (chat)
* Drag handle between panels with `col-resize` cursor
* Constrained to 40–80% left width
* State managed via `leftPercent` with mouse event handlers

### Context System

`PaperReaderContext` is the central state bridge between the paper reader and chat:

```typescript
interface PaperReaderState {
  currentSection: string;
  selectedText: string;
  selectionRect: DOMRect | null;
  viewMode: 'markdown' | 'pdf';

  setCurrentSection(slug: string): void;
  setSelectedText(text: string, rect: DOMRect | null): void;
  setViewMode(mode: ViewMode): void;
  sendToChat(text: string): void;
  scrollToSection(slug: string): void;
  registerChatMethods(methods: ChatMethods): void;
  registerPaperScroll(fn: (slug: string) => void): void;
}
```

The chat panel registers its methods (`setComposerValue`, `focusComposer`, `sendUserMessage`) with the context on mount. The paper panel registers its scroll function. This lets either side trigger actions on the other without direct coupling.

### Custom Hooks

#### `useIntersectionTracker`

Tracks which paper section is currently in the viewport using `IntersectionObserver`. Configured with a root margin of `-20% 0px -70% 0px` so sections are detected when they enter the upper portion of the scroll container.

### Design System

CSS custom properties define the visual language:

| Token            | Value         |
| ---------------- | ------------- |
| `--accent`       | `#2d7a7a`     |
| `--font-heading` | IBM Plex Sans |
| `--font-body`    | Newsreader    |
| `--font-ui`      | DM Sans       |
| `--font-mono`    | SF Mono       |
| `--surface-page` | `#faf9f7`     |
| `--radius-md`    | 6px           |

#### Animations

| Name             | Effect               | Duration  |
| ---------------- | -------------------- | --------- |
| `fade-up`        | Opacity + translateY | 350ms     |
| `chip-enter`     | Scale + translateY   | 300ms     |
| `tooltip-appear` | Opacity + translateY | 120ms     |
| `shimmer`        | Background position  | 1.5s loop |

All animations respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.

### Dev Server

Vite proxies three paths to the backend at `localhost:8321`:

| Path       | Target                        |
| ---------- | ----------------------------- |
| `/chatkit` | Backend ChatKit endpoint      |
| `/figures` | Static figure serving         |
| `/api`     | REST API (paper content, PDF) |
